prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One commonly-praised idea in modern knowledge work that I believe is overrated is the widespread adoption of open-plan offices. While they are often championed as promoting collaboration, creativity, and transparency, the reality is that they can be detrimental to productivity, focus, and employee satisfaction.

The open-plan office concept was popularized to break down physical barriers between coworkers, hoping to foster a sense of community and openness. However, numerous studies have shown that open-plan offices can have the opposite effect. A 2013 study by the University of Sydney, for example, found that open-plan office workers experienced 62% more distractions than those in private offices, and it takes an average of 23 minutes to refocus on a task after an interruption. Constant noise and distractions can lead to stress, decreased motivation, and lowered productivity.

Furthermore, open-plan offices can actually stifle collaboration and creativity. A 2014 Harvard study found that "face-to-face interactions" and "easy access to coworkers" are indeed helpful for "simple or routine work," but for "creative or complex" work, they may hinder productivity. The study discovered that employees in open-plan offices spent 72% less time in "face-to-face interactions" and sent 50% more emails and IMs.

Another downside of open-plan offices is the lack of privacy. The constant observation and feeling of being watched can lead to decreased job satisfaction, lowered motivation, and higher stress levels. A 2013 study published in the journal "Social Science & Medicine" found that people working in open-plan offices took 62% more sick leave than those working in private offices.

In conclusion, while open-plan offices may seem like a good idea in theory, they often lead to more harm than good. To foster collaboration and creativity, employers should instead consider providing a mix of workspaces, including both open and enclosed areas, and give employees the autonomy to choose the environment that best suits their needs and work style.
